description.value: Agent enablement platform that pairs a spec-driven framework with a community registry of reusable skills and context tiles for AI coding agents.

repository.comment: GitHub org; the core CLI (tesslio/cli) ships under a proprietary license, while individual tiles such as tesslio/spec-driven-development-tile are MIT-licensed.

creator.value: Tessl (UK-based startup founded by Guy Podjarny, Snyk founder)

first-release.comment: Company announced July 2024; public products (Spec Registry open beta and Framework closed beta) launched 2025-09-23. CLI repo created 2025-09-12.

latest-release.comment: No tagged releases on CLI; latest push to tesslio/cli 2026-03-05, latest push to spec-driven-development-tile 2026-03-30. Verified 2026-04-21.

license.comment: Core Tessl CLI is proprietary ("Copyright 2025 Tessl. All rights reserved"). The Framework is closed-beta proprietary. Individual tiles such as the spec-driven-development-tile are published under MIT, but the platform itself is proprietary.

pricing-model.comment: Landing page advertises "Get started for free". Spec Registry is in open beta (free); Framework is in closed beta (signup required). No public paid tier pricing published as of 2026-04-21.

open-source.comment: Core CLI and Framework are proprietary. Some methodology tiles are OSS (MIT) but the platform as a whole is not OSI-open-source.

covered-phases.comment: spec-driven-development tile documents four phases (Requirement Gathering, Stakeholder Approval, Implementation, Review). Blog describes the Framework as guiding agents through planning, specification, and implementation with testing. Combined, the workflow covers discovery/requirement gathering, spec authoring, planning, implementation with linked tests, and review.

methodology-style.comment: Martin Fowler's SDD survey describes Tessl as the only one of the three tools (Kiro, Spec-Kit, Tessl) that aspires to spec-anchored and spec-as-source approaches; specs are the primary artifact and guide both agents and code generation.

opinionated.comment: Rules like spec-before-code and one-question-at-a-time are set to "Always Apply"; the workflow prescribes a strict order with explicit stakeholder approval before implementation.

ceremony-level.comment: Structured phases and explicit approval gate, but fewer artifact types than BMAD or Agent-OS. Martin Fowler's article positions Tessl as lighter-weight than spec-kit in file count.

mechanization-level.comment: CLI plus MCP server automates tile installation, spec validation scripts, and context delivery to agents; users invoke via natural-language prompt rather than copy-pasting.

hitl-checkpoints.comment: Explicit Stakeholder Approval phase between spec authoring and implementation.

approval-gates.comment: Rule "spec-before-code": "Never begin implementation without an approved spec." No mandatory plan/tasks/deploy approval is documented.

autonomous-e2e.comment: Stakeholder approval on specs is required by default; described as a human-in-the-loop system.

autonomy-level.comment: Phase-level approval on the spec; within a phase the agent runs autonomously. Mandatory spec approval pushes below the midpoint of 3.

rollback-support.comment: No built-in rollback/undo mechanism documented in the CLI, framework, or spec-driven-development tile. Not set explicitly by the platform as of research date.

supported-agents.comment: docs.tessl.io lists Claude Code, Cursor, Gemini, Codex, Copilot CLI, and Copilot in VS Code. Tessl positions itself as agent-agnostic via MCP; "other" captures Gemini which lacks a dedicated tag.

editor-integrations.comment: CLI-first; VS Code integration via Copilot-in-VSCode. No dedicated JetBrains/Neovim extension documented.

model-agnostic.comment: Works with any MCP-compatible agent; no model lock-in.

mcp-support.comment: CLI doubles as an MCP server; tile files are consumed by the agent's MCP client.

artifact-formats.comment: Specs are .spec.md (Markdown with YAML frontmatter); tile metadata and tessl.json configuration are JSON.

artifacts-covered.comment: Core artifacts are specs (.spec.md) with linked tests ([@test]); the Framework also produces a plan that users review before execution. Tasks are derived from the plan/spec.

project-layout.value: .tessl/ holds installed tile files and rules (auto-aggregated into RULES.md); specs/ holds .spec.md files with YAML frontmatter and [@test] links; tessl.json is the project config.

artifacts-in-git.comment: Specs live alongside source code in the repository and serve as "long-term memory."

language-agnostic.comment: README shows Python and TypeScript examples; targets use glob patterns to any file type.

enforcement-mechanisms.comment: Rules phrased as instructions to the agent; validate-specs.sh and check-spec-links.sh act as linters; [@test] links tie specs to test gates; stakeholder approval is manual; GitHub Actions workflows (tesslio/setup-tessl) run lint/skill-review/version-check on PRs.

strict-phase-ordering.comment: Rule "spec-before-code" with Always Apply = Yes explicitly refuses to advance to implementation without an approved spec.

artifact-validation.comment: scripts/validate-specs.sh validates required YAML frontmatter and structure; scripts/check-spec-links.sh validates that [@test] targets exist.

memory-implementation.comment: Specs and tile rules live as Markdown (and an auto-aggregated RULES.md) in the repo; no vector DB or knowledge graph in the core workflow.

persistent-memory.comment: Specs and tile context persist in the repo across sessions and agents.

project-context-files.comment: .tessl/ directory, tessl.json, and auto-generated RULES.md serve as project-scoped context files.

cost-tracking.comment: No token/cost tracking is documented for the CLI or Framework as of research date.

run-history.comment: No structured run-history or trace feature documented in public materials. Framework is in closed beta, so internal observability may exist but is not publicly confirmed.

telemetry-opt.comment: Telemetry disclosure and opt-out controls not documented in the public docs reviewed.

custom-templates.comment: Users can author their own tiles (skills, rules, docs) and publish to the registry or keep them local; specs are user-authored templates by design.

hooks.comment: No documented lifecycle-event hook API. Rules can be conditionally applied (Always Apply: No) but that is not a user-code hook.

sub-agents.comment: Tiles explicitly define reusable Skills (requirement-gathering, spec-writer, spec-verification, work-review) and users can create their own via the tile-creator tile.

marketplace.comment: Tessl Spec Registry is a public package manager hosting 10,000+ versioned skills and usage specs.

install-mechanism.comment: Primary install: curl -fsSL https://get.tessl.io | sh, or npx @tessl/cli. A Homebrew tap (tesslio/homebrew-tap) also exists.

setup-time.comment: First-run is `tessl init` then `tessl install tessl-labs/spec-driven-development`; completes in under 5 minutes.

self-hosted.comment: CLI runs locally but depends on the hosted Tessl Spec Registry for tile installation. No self-hosted registry option documented.

requires-account.comment: Installing tiles via the CLI does not document an account requirement; open-beta registry appears to allow anonymous reads. The Framework is in closed beta and requires a signup (Typeform) for access, but the core registry/CLI workflow does not.

git-worktree-support.comment: No documented git worktree integration in CLI or framework materials reviewed.

documentation-quality.comment: Dedicated docs site, detailed tile READMEs with workflow diagrams, nine eval scenarios, and a public registry with 10k+ specs. Framework itself is closed beta so public docs are thinner there.

github-stars.comment: Star count on the primary repository tesslio/cli as of 2026-04-21. The spec-driven-development-tile has 22 additional stars; other tiles/skills are in the low single digits.

active-maintenance.comment: Latest push to tesslio/cli 2026-03-05; tesslio/spec-driven-development-tile 2026-03-30; tesslio/good-oss-citizen 2026-04-19. All within 90 days of 2026-04-21.

example-projects.comment: Nine eval scenarios in the spec-driven-development tile; README demonstrates combining methodology tile with library tiles (Quarkus). Registry holds 10k+ usage examples.

community-channel.comment: Tessl Discord linked from the tile README: https://discord.com/invite/jbb2vHnHZQ
